attributes.stem:
A 75-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a six-month history of urinary leakage when coughing, sneezing, or lifting heavy objects. She also describes episodes of needing to rush to the toilet and sometimes leaking before she can get there. She reports wearing pads daily and feels this is significantly affecting her quality of life. She does not report any concerns about a vaginal bulge or dragging sensation. She denies any dysuria. Her medical history includes osteoarthritis and type 2 diabetes, and previous hysterectomy at age 52. She had two vaginal deliveries of 4.1kg and 4.2kg babies 50 and 52 years ago. Her regular medications include metformin and paracetamol. She has never smoked.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Abdominal examination reveals no masses or tenderness. There is evidence of mild anterior and posterior compartment prolapse on examination.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Surgical repair of pelvic organ prolapse
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with symptoms of mixed urinary incontinence, with features of both stress incontinence (leakage with coughing, sneezing, lifting) and urge incontinence (rushing to the toilet, leaking before arrival). Given this mixed picture, further investigation is needed before committing to a specific treatment approach.

Urodynamic studies are the most appropriate next step as they can objectively assess bladder and urethral function, differentiating between the stress and urge components of this patient's mixed incontinence. These studies measure pressures in the bladder during filling and emptying, assess urethral closure pressure, and can identify detrusor overactivity. The results will guide the most appropriate treatment strategy, whether that be focused on the stress component, the urge component, or both.

Mid urethral sling is a surgical procedure primarily used for stress urinary incontinence. While this patient does have stress incontinence symptoms, she also has significant urge symptoms. Proceeding directly to surgery without urodynamic confirmation of the predominant type of incontinence could lead to suboptimal outcomes, especially if there is a significant urge component that would not be addressed by the sling. Additionally, mid urethral slings are now only performed by urogynaecologists after extensive counselling due to potential mesh complications.

Oxybutynin is an antimuscarinic medication primarily used for urge incontinence. While it might help with the urge component of this patient's symptoms, it would not address the stress component. Additionally, it can cause significant side effects in older adults, including dry mouth, constipation, and cognitive impairment.

Surgical repair of pelvic organ prolapse would not be appropriate as the patient has no symptoms of prolapse and despite the examination revealing mild evidence of anterior and posterior compartment prolapse. Surgery for prolapse should only be performed in symptomatic patients and may actually worsen incontinence symptoms in some patients.

Duloxetine is sometimes used for stress urinary incontinence, but evidence for its efficacy is limited. It would not address the urge component of this patient's mixed incontinence. Furthermore, it can have significant side effects including nausea, dizziness, and fatigue.

Once urodynamic studies have clarified the predominant type of incontinence, treatment can be tailored appropriately. For stress incontinence, conservative management includes pelvic floor muscle training, weight loss if appropriate, and topical oestrogen cream which can improve urethral coaptation and tissue quality in postmenopausal women. Surgical options include mid urethral slings, though these require careful patient selection and counselling. For urge incontinence, bladder training and lifestyle modifications are first-line treatments. Pharmacological options include antimuscarinics like oxybutynin, though in older adults, mirabegron (a beta-3 adrenergic agonist) often has a better tolerance profile with fewer anticholinergic side effects such as dry mouth, constipation, and cognitive impairment. For mixed incontinence, treatment typically targets the predominant component first, though some patients may require combination therapy addressing both components.
learningPoints: Urodynamic studies are indicated for patients with mixed urinary incontinence to guide appropriate management.
Mixed urinary incontinence presents with both stress and urge incontinence symptoms and requires careful assessment before treatment.
Pharmacological and surgical treatments for mixed urinary incontinence should be guided by urodynamic findings to ensure optimal outcomes.
references: Royal Australian College of General Practitioners. Urinary incontinence. In: RACGP aged care clinical guide (Silver Book) – Part A. East Melbourne: RACGP; 2019. Available from: https://www.racgp.org.au/clinical-resources/clinical-guidelines/key-racgp-guidelines/view-all-racgp-guidelines/silver-book/part-a/urinary-incontinence

Harris S, Leslie SW, Riggs J. Mixed urinary incontinence. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan–. [cited 2025 Apr 30]. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK534234/
